text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through the palmar point and places lateral ports, revealing extensive omental and bowel adhesions from the xiphoid process to the pelvis. Sharp scissor dissection is used for adhesiolysis, staying close to the abdominal wall, aided by pneumodissection. Harmonic shears are then employed to continue adhesiolysis near the anterior abdominal wall, exposing small herniated defects and previous sutures.

The surgeon carefully reduces the bowel contents from a large herniated defect using gentle traction, counter traction, and rotatory movements. The bowel is densely adhered to the abdominal wall, requiring sharp dissection with scissors without energy sources to avoid bleeding. The previous sutures are visible, showing dense adhesion of the bowel to the abdominal wall.
Again, very, very slow and meticulous dissection is required and patience is very, very important while contemplating an adduciolysis of this level.

The surgeon identifies a herniated defect during adhesiolysis. Several enterotomies are encountered and repaired laparoscopically, with sutures applied immediately to prevent losing track of the enterotomy. After closing the enterotomy, the surgeon resumes adhesiolysis.

Another herniated defect is visualized, and the surgeon reduces the bowel contents completely. The adhesions are visible, extending from the upper to lower abdomen, with multiple defects clearly observable.

The surgeon observes the previous sutures and bites on the underlying bowel, noting the dense bowel adhesions. A large enterotomy is visible, and the surgeon uses 3-0 Vicryl to close the enterotomy defect with intermittent sutures, ensuring not to take deep bites to prevent future bowel strictures.
The surgeon continues sharp adhesiolysis, progressing towards the pelvis. The adhesiolysis is nearly complete, having encountered and addressed two enterotomies during the procedure.

The panoramic view shows the completed adhesiolysis with visible Swiss cheese defects and previous suture bites on the underlying bowel. The surgeon uses non-absorbable barbed sutures to close the defect, avoiding mesh placement due to the encountered enterotomies.
The surgeon completes the laparoscopic anatomical repair of the hernia by closing the hernia defects. The surgical field is inspected for hemostasis, marking the end of the procedure.
